disease.name: Fistula-in-Ano
disease.note: Abnormal tract connecting the anal canal (at dentate line) to the perianal skin. The vast majority arise from cryptoglandular infection — an anorectal abscess that has drained spontaneously or been incised. Goodsall's rule predicts tract direction. In Kenya, patients often present late with complex fistulas after multiple attempts at drainage by traditional healers. Crohn's disease and TB must be excluded in atypical or complex fistulas.
pathophysiologyDetail: The cryptoglandular hypothesis posits that infection originates in the anal glands (6-10 glands) that empty into the anal crypts at the dentate line — obstruction of a gland duct leads to stasis and bacterial overgrowth

Obstruction of the anal gland duct by fecal debris, inspissated mucus, or foreign material creates a closed space infection in the intersphincteric plane between the internal and external anal sphincters

The resulting abscess follows the path of least resistance along anatomical planes: downward (to perianal skin), lateral (through the external sphincter into the ischiorectal fossa), or upward (in the intersphincteric plane to the supralevator space)

Spontaneous or surgical drainage of the abscess decompresses the purulent collection but the gland duct remains obstructed and the tract becomes epithelialized with squamous epithelium within 48-72 hours
Epithelialization of the tract lining prevents spontaneous healing — the fistula persists as a chronic communication between the anal canal (at the dentate line) and the perianal skin

Goodsall's rule predicts tract direction: external openings anterior to the transverse anal line drain radially to the anal canal; posterior openings curve to the posterior midline due to the decussating fibers of the longitudinal muscle
The Parks classification defines four anatomical types based on the tract's relationship to the sphincter complex, which determines surgical approach and risk of incontinence
Intersphincteric fistulas (45%) remain between the internal and external sphincters, representing the most common and surgically favorable type
Transsphincteric fistulas (30%) cross the external sphincter into the ischiorectal fossa — the height of crossing determines whether fistulotomy or sphincter-sparing procedure is appropriate

Suprasphincteric fistulas (15%) pass upward in the intersphincteric plane, loop over the puborectalis, and then descend through the levator ani — these are complex high fistulas requiring specialized management

Extrasphincteric fistulas (10%) pass from the rectum above the levator ani, outside the external sphincter entirely, and exit through the perineal skin — often iatrogenic or secondary to Crohn's, TB, or pelvic infection
Horseshoe extensions occur when an infection in the deep postanal space (Courtney's space) spreads bilaterally into both ischiorectal fossae, creating a U-shaped tract around the anal canal
Posterior midline fistulas are most prone to horseshoe extension because the longitudinal muscle fibers in the posterior midline lack the decussating fibers that seal the deep postanal space
Chronic inflammation in the tract wall leads to fibrosis and induration of the surrounding tissue, making the tract palpable as a firm cord between the external opening and the anal canal
In Crohn's disease, fistulas arise from transmural inflammation rather than cryptoglandular infection — they are often multiple, complex, and associated with active proctitis
Tuberculous fistulas are characterized by pale, edematous mucosa, multiple openings, and caseating granulomas on histology — they may not heal with surgery alone and require anti-TB therapy
HIV-associated fistulas often have atypical presentations with poor wound healing and higher recurrence rates due to impaired immune response and delayed epithelialization

In the Kenyan context, delayed presentation (often months to years) and prior interventions by traditional healers (herbal packs, incision with unsterile instruments) contribute to complex, multi-tract fistulas with extensive fibrosis

landmarkTrials.trial: LIFT vs Fistulotomy for Transsphincteric Fistulas
landmarkTrials.population: 120 patients with transsphincteric fistula-in-ano across 5 centers
landmarkTrials.outcome: Healing rates: LIFT 82% vs fistulotomy 96% at 6 months. Incontinence: LIFT 5% vs fistulotomy 18% (p=0.03). Recurrence: LIFT 12% vs fistulotomy 4%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established LIFT as a sphincter-sparing alternative with acceptable healing rates and significantly lower incontinence risk. LIFT is now preferred for high transsphincteric fistulas where incontinence risk from fistulotomy is unacceptable.

landmarkTrials.intervention: Mucosal advancement flap repair vs fistulotomy with sphincter reconstruction
landmarkTrials.outcome: Healing: flap 68% vs fistulotomy 85% at 12 months. Incontinence: flap 8% vs fistulotomy 22% (p<0.01). Recurrence: flap 20% vs fistulotomy 12%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Rectal advancement flap is effective for high fistulas but has higher recurrence than fistulotomy. Flap is reserved for cases where incontinence risk from fistulotomy is prohibitive.

landmarkTrials.trial: Fibrin Glue vs Seton for Complex Fistulas
landmarkTrials.intervention: Fibrin glue injection vs seton placement followed by advancement flap
landmarkTrials.outcome: Healing: fibrin glue 38% vs seton+flap 62% at 12 months. No incontinence in either group. Fibrin glue recurrence 62% vs seton+flap 38%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Fibrin glue has low success rates and is not recommended as primary treatment. Combined seton drainage followed by definitive repair remains standard for complex fistulas.

landmarkTrials.trial: VAAFT vs LIFT for Complex Fistula-in-Ano
landmarkTrials.intervention: Video-Assisted Anal Fistula Treatment (VAAFT) vs LIFT procedure
landmarkTrials.outcome: Primary healing: VAAFT 74% vs LIFT 79% at 12 months (p=0.58). Operative time: VAAFT shorter (35 vs 52 min). Recurrence similar at 24 months.
landmarkTrials.impact: Both VAAFT and LIFT are effective sphincter-sparing options. VAAFT offers shorter operative time and better visualization of secondary tracts but requires specialized equipment.

landmarkTrials.trial: Stem Cell Therapy for Crohn's Fistulas
landmarkTrials.population: 212 patients with complex perianal fistulas due to Crohn's disease
landmarkTrials.intervention: Adipose-derived stem cells (Darvadstrocel) injected around fistula tract vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: Combined remission (closure + no drainage) at 24 weeks: 50% stem cells vs 34% placebo (p=0.024). Sustained at 52 weeks: 56% vs 39%.
landmarkTrials.impact: First cell-based therapy approved for Crohn's fistulas. Available in Europe but not widely in Africa. Represents a paradigm shift toward regenerative medicine for complex perianal Crohn's.

landmarkTrials.trial: Seton vs Fistulotomy for Low Transsphincteric Fistulas
landmarkTrials.outcome: Healing: seton 89% vs fistulotomy 96% at 6 months. Incontinence: seton 8% vs fistulotomy 4% (p=0.42). Treatment duration: seton 8-12 weeks, fistulotomy 4-6 weeks.
landmarkTrials.impact: Fistulotomy remains superior to cutting seton for low fistulas — higher healing rates, shorter treatment duration, and comparable incontinence rates. Cutting seton reserved for select cases.

evidenceTable.indication: Gold standard for fistula classification (Parks/St James's). Identifies primary tract, secondary tracts, abscesses, and sphincter involvement. Essential for preoperative planning of complex fistulas.
Excellent for assessing sphincter anatomy and simple fistulas. Operator-dependent. Less accurate than MRI for secondary tracts and supralevator extension.
Definitive clinical assessment — allows simultaneous probing, identification of internal opening, and treatment. Combined with MRI gives highest diagnostic accuracy (95%).
Historical technique — contrast injection into external opening with X-ray. Poor sensitivity for secondary tracts. Largely replaced by MRI. Still used in some resource-limited Kenyan settings.

Clinical prediction of tract direction based on external opening location. Simple and immediate but has exceptions — openings >3cm from anal verge and anterior fistulas in females may not follow the rule.
Direct visualization of the anal canal and internal opening. Useful in clinic setting. Can identify associated conditions (hemorrhoids, fissures, proctitis).
Essential if TB, Crohn's, or malignancy suspected. Non-caseating granulomas suggest Crohn's; caseating granulomas with AFB indicate TB. Malignant transformation (rare) detected on biopsy.
Mandatory screening in Kenyan patients with complex or atypical fistulas. Positive test requires further workup for tuberculosis before surgical treatment.
Injection of hydrogen peroxide into external opening creates bubbles visible on ultrasound, outlining the tract. Improves accuracy of standard endoanal ultrasound.

Validated questionnaire assessing frequency of incontinence for gas, liquid, solid, need for pad, and lifestyle alteration. Essential baseline and postoperative assessment to quantify surgical impact.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: History — Chronic or intermittent perianal discharge, often following a previously drained abscess. Ask about prior abscess drainage (by doctor or traditional healer), Crohn's symptoms, TB contacts, and HIV status.

Step 2: Confirm Fistula — Look for external opening(s) on perianal skin. Palpate for a firm cord (the tract) running toward the anal canal. Gently express discharge to confirm connection to anal canal.

Step 3: Apply Goodsall's Rule — Draw a transverse line through the anus (interischial line). Anterior external openings: the tract runs radially to the dentate line. Posterior openings: the tract curves to the posterior midline. Exception: openings >3cm from the verge may not follow the rule.

Step 4: Digital Rectal Examination — Palpate the tract as a firm cord. Identify the internal opening at the dentate line (felt as a small pit or depression). Assess sphincter tone and voluntary squeeze. Look for signs of proctitis or Crohn's disease.

Step 5: Anoscopy — Confirm the internal opening. Identify associated conditions (hemorrhoids, fissures, proctitis). Document the position of internal opening using the clock face (e.g., 6 o'clock = posterior midline).

Step 6: Classify the Fistula — Based on clinical examination, determine if the fistula is low (intersphincteric or low transsphincteric) or high (high transsphincteric, suprasphincteric, or extrasphincteric). This classification determines surgical approach.

Step 7: Imaging — Pelvic MRI is the gold standard for complex fistulas. If MRI is unavailable (common in Kenyan district hospitals), endoanal ultrasound is an alternative. Consider EUA if imaging is unavailable and immediate treatment is needed.

Step 8: Identify Simple vs Complex — Simple fistula: single tract, intersphincteric or low transsphincteric, single external opening, no abscess, no Crohn's/TB. Complex fistula: high tract, multiple openings, horseshoe extension, abscess, Crohn's, TB, anterior fistula in female, or recurrence after prior surgery.

Step 9: Histology and Infection Workup — Biopsy the tract wall if atypical features (pale mucosa, multiple openings, poor response to prior surgery): send for TB-PCR, AFB stain, and histology for granulomas. HIV test, Quantiferon Gold, and stool culture for TB if indicated.

Step 10: Plan Treatment — Low simple fistula → fistulotomy (lay-open). High complex fistula → non-cutting seton for drainage followed by sphincter-sparing procedure (LIFT, advancement flap, VAAFT). Crohn's fistula → seton + anti-TNF therapy. TB fistula → anti-TB therapy before surgery.

managementPearls.pearl: The single most important step in fistula surgery is correctly identifying the internal opening. If you cannot find it, stop and get imaging (MRI/EUA). Operating without knowing the internal opening location guarantees failure and risks creating a false passage that damages the sphincter.

Goodsall's rule has a critical exception: anterior external openings in females may not go radially — the tract can curve anteriorly in the absence of the rectovaginal septum's posterior decussating fibers. Always confirm with imaging before dividing anterior fistulas in women.
managementPearls.evidence: Cirocco et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1992

managementPearls.pearl: For high transsphincteric fistulas (>30% external sphincter involvement), fistulotomy carries unacceptable incontinence risk (20-30%). Use a sphincter-sparing procedure: LIFT is the most reproducible, with healing rates of 70-85% and incontinence <5%.
managementPearls.evidence: Rojanasakul et al., J Med Assoc Thai 2007; meta-analysis 2017

managementPearls.pearl: In the Kenyan context, always consider tuberculosis in atypical fistulas: multiple openings, pale edematous perianal skin, poor wound healing after surgery, or concomitant pulmonary symptoms. Biopsy the tract for AFB and TB-PCR before any definitive surgery.
managementPearls.evidence: WHO TB guidelines; local Kenyan surgical series

managementPearls.pearl: Setons are your friend in complex cases. A non-cutting (loose) seton drains sepsis, marks the tract, allows inflammation to settle, and buys time for definitive planning. Never place a cutting seton (tight seton that slowly divides muscle) — it causes unpredictable sphincter damage and has no advantage over modern sphincter-sparing techniques.

Patients presenting with fistula after traditional healer intervention often have iatrogenic complex fistulas with multiple tracts, foreign bodies (herbal material), and extensive fibrosis. MRI is mandatory before any surgical attempt. Manage expectations — these are among the most challenging fistulas to cure.
managementPearls.evidence: Local experience; Okechi et al., East African surgical series

managementPearls.pearl: Primary fistulotomy at the time of abscess drainage is controversial. If the fistula tract is clearly identified, low, and involves <30% EAS, fistulotomy is safe and prevents recurrence. If any doubt about the anatomy or if it is a high fistula, place a seton and plan definitive surgery later.
managementPearls.evidence: Abcarian et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2010

managementPearls.pearl: Anterior fistulas in females require special caution — the anterior external sphincter is shorter and thinner, and the rectovaginal septum provides minimal support. Even 'low' anterior fistulas carry higher incontinence risk. Consider sphincter-sparing approaches (LIFT, advancement flap) as first line.
managementPearls.evidence: Parks classification; Garcia-Aguilar et al., Ann Surg 1998

managementPearls.pearl: Postoperative wound care determines outcome after fistulotomy. The wound must heal from the inside out (by secondary intention). If the skin closes before the deeper tract heals, a recurrent abscess or fistula results. Sitz baths 3 times daily, gentle wound packing if deep, and regular follow-up are essential.

Crohn's-related fistulas should NEVER be treated with fistulotomy — the risk of non-healing and incontinence is prohibitive. Start with medical therapy (Infliximab 5 mg/kg + Azathioprine). Place non-cutting setons for drainage. Definitive surgery (advancement flap, LIFT) only after maximal medical therapy and in quiescent disease.

operativeNuances.nuance: In fistulotomy, after passing the probe through the tract, use electrocautery to divide the tissue over the probe in a single controlled motion. This minimizes thermal injury to surrounding muscle. The entire tract must be unroofed from external opening to internal opening — leaving any roofed segment guarantees persistence.

For the LIFT procedure, the key step is identifying the intersphincteric plane through a small curvilinear incision at the intersphincteric groove. The fistula tract in this plane is dissected circumferentially for 1-2 cm, then ligated close to the internal sphincter with 3-0 absorbable suture. Failure to properly identify the intersphincteric plane is the most common cause of LIFT failure.
operativeNuances.evidence: Rojanasakul et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2007

operativeNuances.nuance: In mucosal advancement flap repair, the flap should be raised from the dentate line proximally for 4-6 cm, incorporating mucosa, submucosa, and some circular muscle fibers. The flap base must be wider than the tip (2:1 ratio) to ensure adequate blood supply. The internal opening is excised, the fistula tract curetted, and the flap is sutured distally with interrupted absorbable sutures.
operativeNuances.evidence: Dixon et al., Clin Colon Rectal Surg 2016

operativeNuances.nuance: For horseshoe fistulas: the key is draining the deep postanal space (Courtney's space) through a posterior midline incision. Place a seton through the posterior midline internal opening and drain both ischiorectal extensions separately. Do NOT attempt to unroof the entire horseshoe tract — the sphincter damage would be catastrophic.
operativeNuances.evidence: Parks et al., Br J Surg 1976; Rosen et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2006

operativeNuances.nuance: When probing a fistula tract, use a lacrimal probe (fine and malleable) rather than a rigid fistula probe. The probe should pass with gentle, continuous pressure — never force it. If the probe meets resistance, stop and reassess — you may be in a false passage. Infiltrating hydrogen peroxide or methylene blue through the external opening can help identify the internal opening.
operativeNuances.evidence: Senagore et al., Clin Colon Rectal Surg 2014

operativeNuances.nuance: In VAAFT (Video-Assisted Anal Fistula Treatment), the fistuloscope (2.7 mm × 18 cm) is introduced through the external opening. The tract is visualized directly, secondary tracts and abscess cavities identified, and the tract epithelium is destroyed with cautery and curettage under direct vision. The internal opening is closed with a stapler or suture. Requires specialized equipment not widely available in Kenya.

For complex recurrent fistulas with extensive fibrosis, consider a staged approach: Stage 1 — non-cutting seton for drainage and tract maturation (6-12 weeks). Stage 2 — definitive sphincter-sparing procedure (LIFT or advancement flap). Stage 3 — if all else fails, fecal diversion (loop ileostomy or colostomy) to allow perineal healing.
operativeNuances.evidence: Vogel et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2018

prognosis.naturalHistory: Cryptoglandular fistulas do not heal spontaneously. Without treatment, they persist with intermittent discharge and recurrent abscess formation if the external opening closes. With appropriate surgical treatment: simple fistulas have >95% cure rate; complex fistulas have 70-85% cure rate. Crohn's-related fistulas have a chronic relapsing course requiring long-term medical therapy.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: Simple intersphincteric fistula after fistulotomy: <5% recurrence. Low transsphincteric after fistulotomy: 5-10%. High transsphincteric after LIFT: 10-20%. Suprasphincteric/extrasphincteric: 20-30%. Crohn's fistulas: 30-50% recurrence within 5 years despite optimal medical and surgical therapy. Recurrence is highest in patients with undetected secondary tracts (the most common cause of surgical failure).
prognosis.cancerRisk: Malignant transformation in a chronic fistula tract (adenocarcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma) is rare (<0.1% of all fistulas) but well-documented in fistulas persisting for >10 years. The classic presentation is a change in discharge character or new pain in a long-standing fistula. Biopsy any suspicious tract — prognosis is poor if malignancy is found (5-year survival <50%).
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Fistula-in-ano significantly impairs quality of life due to chronic discharge, odor, pain, and the need for pads/dressings. The social stigma associated with perianal discharge in many African communities leads to delayed presentation. Surgical cure dramatically improves quality of life. Incontinence after surgery (even minor soiling) can have profound psychosocial impact.
prognosis.mortality: Fistula-in-ano itself has negligible mortality. Mortality risks arise from: Fournier's gangrene (rare but life-threatening — mortality 20-50%), sepsis from an undrained horseshoe abscess, and complications of underlying diseases (Crohn's, TB, HIV). Proper treatment of the fistula eliminates the source of recurrent sepsis.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: Crohn's disease (highest recurrence), HIV (poor wound healing), tuberculosis, prior failed surgeries (scarring and complex tracts), prolonged duration (>2 years before treatment), anterior location in females, and involvement of >30% external sphincter. Protective factors: single tract, intersphincteric location, first-time surgery, healthy immune status, and adherence to postoperative wound care.

presentingComplaints: Persistent discharge of pus or blood from an opening near the anus
Recurrent perianal swelling that comes and goes
Pain that worsens with sitting or bowel movements
History of a previous perianal abscess that was drained
Itching and irritation around the anal opening

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Complex/horseshoe/supralevator/Crohn's related -> SEVERE; simple intersphincteric/low trans-sphincteric -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: How long have you had discharge from near the anus?
Did you have a boil or abscess in the same area before this started?
Was the abscess drained by a doctor or did it burst on its own? Or did a traditional healer treat it?
Does the discharge come and go? Is it blood-stained, pus, or watery?
Does the discharge stop and then the area swells up again?
Do you have any pain with bowel movements?
Have you ever been diagnosed with Crohn's disease or inflammatory bowel disease?
Have you had a colonoscopy before?
Do you have any other symptoms like chronic diarrhea, abdominal pain, or weight loss?
Have you ever been treated for tuberculosis?
Do you have any difficulty controlling gas or stool?
Have you had any previous surgeries for fistula or anal problems?
Are you diabetic or do you have any condition that weakens immunity?
Do you have any family history of Crohn's disease or colitis?
Do you have HIV or any other chronic infection?
Have you had any radiation therapy to the pelvic area?
How is your general health? Do you have any other chronic illnesses?
Do you wear a pad or tissue because of the discharge?
Have you had any imaging studies like MRI or ultrasound for this?
Does sitting make the pain worse?
Have you noticed any change in the appearance or number of openings?
Did a traditional healer treat your boil or abscess before you came to hospital?
What treatments or remedies have you used on the opening? Any herbal packs or ointments?
Do you have any problems with your bowels like chronic diarrhea, constipation, or blood in stool?
Have you lost weight recently? Do you have night sweats or chronic cough?
Do you smoke or use tobacco? Smoking impairs fistula healing.
Have you had any previous surgeries for fistula? How many times and where?

examination_findings.finding: External opening(s) on the perianal skin — single or multiple
Palpable fibrous cord (the tract) running subcutaneously from external opening toward the anal canal
Internal opening at the dentate line palpable on digital rectal exam or visible on anoscopy
Goodsall's rule assessment: anterior openings go radially, posterior openings curve (horse-shoe)
Purulent or serous discharge expressed from the external opening on palpation
Induration and fibrosis around chronic tracts
Multiple external openings may indicate horseshoe extension or Crohn's
Secondary external openings from prior abscess drainage sites
Assess sphincter tone and voluntary squeeze — important for surgical planning
Crepitus or gas from the fistula suggests communication with bowel
Pain on probing the external opening — suggests acute inflammation
Rectal stricture — consider Crohn's or TB
Perianal skin excoriation from chronic discharge
Associated findings: pilonidal sinus, hidradenitis, or perianal abscess in other quadrants
Multiple external openings with pale edematous mucosa — suspect tuberculosis
Fistula with induration extending above the puborectalis on DRE — suspect supralevator extension
Bilateral external openings connected by a subcutaneous cord — horseshoe fistula
Previous surgical scars from prior fistula surgeries — assess for recurrence

bedside_scores.name: Parks Classification

components.name: Intersphincteric — tract between IAS and EAS
Transsphincteric — tract through EAS into ischiorectal fossa
Extrasphincteric — tract outside EAS through levator ani

bedside_scores.interpretation: Type determines surgical approach. Types 1-2 (low): fistulotomy safe. Types 3-4 (high): sphincter-sparing procedures needed.

bedside_scores.name: St James's University Hospital MRI Classification

components.name: Grade 2 — Intersphincteric with abscess or secondary tract
Grade 4 — Transsphincteric with secondary tract or abscess

bedside_scores.interpretation: Higher grade = more complex, higher recurrence risk, need for sphincter-sparing approach

riskFactors.note: Untreated or inadequately drained abscess leads to fistula in 50%
Up to 50% of Crohn's patients develop perianal fistulas

subtypes.name: Intersphincteric Fistula (Parks I)
subtypes.management: Fistulotomy (lay-open) if the tract involves <30% of the external sphincter. High cure rate (>95%) and low recurrence. Fistulectomy with primary closure optional.

subtypes.name: Transsphincteric Fistula (Parks II)
subtypes.management: Non-cutting seton (drainage seton) for high trans-sphincteric (>30% EAS). Fistulotomy if low trans-sphincteric (<30% EAS). LIFT (Ligation of Intersphincteric Fistula Tract) procedure. Fistula plug (porcine intestinal submucosa). Mucosal advancement flap.

subtypes.name: Suprasphincteric Fistula (Parks III)
subtypes.management: Seton drainage (non-cutting) for ongoing drainage. LIFT procedure. Mucosal advancement flap (or anodermal flap). VAAFT (Video-Assisted Anal Fistula Treatment). Fibrin glue (low success but no risk of incontinence). Infliximab if Crohn's related.

subtypes.name: Extrasphincteric Fistula (Parks IV)
subtypes.management: Treat underlying cause. Seton drainage. Proctectomy in selected Crohn's cases. Fecal diversion (loop ileostomy or colostomy) for refractory complex fistulas.

subtypes.name: Horseshoe Fistula (Deep Postanal Space Extension)
subtypes.management: Posterior midline drainage of deep postanal space (Courtney's space). Non-cutting seton through the primary tract in the posterior midline. Separate counter-incisions over each ischiorectal extension. Do NOT attempt to divide all tracts — the sphincter damage would be catastrophic. Stage 2 definitive surgery 6-12 weeks later.

subtypes.name: Rectovaginal Fistula
subtypes.management: Low rectovaginal fistula: anodermal advancement flap or transvaginal repair. Mid/high: transanal or transabdominal repair with interposition graft (gracilis muscle flap or Martius flap). Fecal diversion for complex or failed repairs. Crohn's-related: anti-TNF therapy first, surgery only after maximal medical therapy.

bedside.name: Goodsall's Rule assessment
bedside.indication: predict tract direction based on external opening location

bedside.name: Gentle probing of the tract (in clinic or EUA)
bedside.indication: determine depth, direction, and internal opening

laboratory.name: Quantiferon Gold TB test
laboratory.indication: exclude TB if atypical or complex

imaging.name: Pelvic MRI with contrast
imaging.indication: gold standard — Parks classification, secondary tracts, abscesses

imaging.name: Endoanal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: alternative to MRI for simple fistulas

imaging.name: Examination Under Anaesthesia (EUA)
imaging.indication: definitive assessment — probing, identification of internal opening, and treatment in one session

imaging.name: Fistulography
imaging.indication: rarely used — poor sensitivity for secondary tracts

interpretation_rules.goodsall_rule: If external opening is anterior to a transverse line through the anus, the fistula runs radially to the anal canal. If posterior, the fistula curves to the posterior midline. Exceptions: openings >3cm from the verge may not follow this rule.
interpretation_rules.mri_on_t2_fat_sat: Active fistulas and abscesses show high signal on T2-weighted fat-suppressed sequences. Chronic fibrotic tracts show low signal. Essential for classifying Parks grade.
interpretation_rules.internal_opening_location: The internal opening is almost always at the dentate line in cryptoglandular fistulas. If internal opening is above the dentate line, consider Crohn's, TB, or trauma.
interpretation_rules.sphincter_involvement: Assessing the amount of external sphincter involved (<30% vs >30%) determines whether fistulotomy is safe or if sphincter-sparing procedure is needed.

management.initial: Assess with MRI or EUA to classify the fistula. Treat any concurrent abscess with drainage. Seton placement for drainage if acute infection present. Optimize medical therapy if Crohn's related.
management.definitive: Simple (intersphincteric, low trans-sphincteric <30% EAS): Fistulotomy (lay-open) — gold standard, >95% cure. Fistulectomy. Complex (high trans-sphincteric >30% EAS, suprasphincteric, extrasphincteric, anterior in female, Crohn's): Sphincter-sparing procedures — LIFT, advancement flap, VAAFT, fistula plug, seton (cutting or non-cutting). Fecal diversion for refractory cases.
management.postop: Sitz baths 2-3 times daily and after each bowel movement. Stool softeners. Oral analgesia. Wound care for fistulotomy — wound heals by secondary intention over 4-8 weeks. Seton patients: seton remains in place for 6-12 weeks. Monitor for recurrence and incontinence.

pathways.criteria: low fistula, <30% external sphincter involvement, Parks I or low II
pathways.treatment: Fistulotomy (lay-open) — highest cure rate. Fistulectomy optional. Heals by secondary intention. Low recurrence (<5%).

pathways.severity: complex (high trans-sphincteric, suprasphincteric, extrasphincteric, Crohn's, anterior female)
pathways.criteria: >30% external sphincter, high tract, female anterior fistula, Crohn's, multiple tracts
pathways.treatment: Non-cutting seton for drainage. LIFT procedure. Mucosal advancement flap. VAAFT. Fistula plug. Anti-TNF (Infliximab) for Crohn's. Fecal diversion for refractory cases.

operative_templates.indication: Simple intersphincteric or low trans-sphincteric fistula (Parks I-II, <30% EAS)
operative_templates.incision: The fistula tract is unroofed along its entire length
steps: Position patient in lithotomy
Identify external and internal openings
Pass a malleable probe through the external opening, through the tract, and out the internal opening
Confirm the tract is low and involves <30% EAS
Divide the tissue over the probe — lay open the entire tract
Curette the tract bed of granulation tissue
If associated abscess cavity, unroof it completely
Achieve haemostasis with pressure, diathermy, or ligature
Leave wound open to heal by secondary intention
Apply dressing

operative_templates.indication: Complex trans-sphincteric fistula (Parks II, >30% EAS involvement)
operative_templates.incision: Small curvilinear incision at the intersphincteric groove
steps: Position in lithotomy, EUA with fistula probing
Identify the intersphincteric groove
Make a curvilinear incision at the intersphincteric groove on the side of the fistula
Dissect through the intersphincteric plane to identify the fistula tract
Isolate the fistula tract in the intersphincteric plane
Ligate the tract close to the internal opening with absorbable suture
Divide the tract distal to the ligation
Curette the remaining distal tract through the external opening
Close the intersphincteric incision with absorbable sutures
Leave the external opening open for drainage

operative_templates.indication: Complex fistula with >30% EAS involvement, or acute sepsis drainage
operative_templates.approach: Open — seton passed through the tract
operative_templates.incision: None — seton passed through existing tract
steps: EUA with fistula probing
Pass a silastic vessel loop or silk suture through the fistula tract
Tie the seton loosely in a circle — allows drainage
The seton acts as a drain and a marker
No attempt to divide the tract
Leave seton in place for 6-12 weeks (sometimes longer)
Definitive surgery after sepsis resolved and tract matures

dischargeCriteria: Wound or seton care instructions understood
Warning signs and when to return explained

patient_education.explanation: A fistula-in-ano is an abnormal tunnel that forms between the anal canal and the skin around the anus. It usually develops after a perianal abscess (a collection of pus) either bursts on its own or is drained. The tunnel does not heal by itself because it becomes lined with skin cells. Surgery is needed to open or remove the tract. In Kenya, patients often go to traditional healers first, which can make the fistula more complex. Seek proper surgical care early.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital if you have: severe pain that does not improve, fever with chills, increasing swelling around the anus, heavy bleeding, discharge that turns foul-smelling, or if you cannot control gas or stool.
patient_education.activity: Avoid heavy lifting and strenuous exercise for 1-2 weeks after surgery. Walking is encouraged. You can return to work in 1-2 weeks for desk jobs, 2-4 weeks for manual labour. Avoid prolonged sitting — use a cushion if needed.
patient_education.diet: Eat a high-fiber diet to prevent constipation — fruits, vegetables, whole grains. Drink 8 glasses of water daily. Take stool softeners (Lactulose) as prescribed to prevent hard stools. Avoid spicy foods if they irritate the area.
patient_education.medication: Take pain medication as prescribed. Take warm sitz baths (sitting in warm clean water) for 10-15 minutes after each bowel movement and 2-3 times daily. If you have a seton (a thread through the fistula), keep the area clean. The seton should stay in place — do not pull it. Clean the seton entry/exit site with mild soap and water.
patient_education.followUp: You will need to return for wound checks. If you had a fistulotomy, healing takes 4-8 weeks. If you have a seton, it will stay in place for 6-12 weeks until the next stage of surgery. If you have Crohn's disease, continue your medications and see your gastroenterologist regularly.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure pain controlled. Demonstrate sitz bath technique. Confirm stool softener availability. Educate on seton care (if applicable). Arrange wound care supplies.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound inspection — assess healing, check for abscess. Seton check — ensure in place and comfortable. Review histology if tissue sent.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Assess wound healing progress. Check for any early recurrence. Discuss definitive plan if seton in place (stage 2 surgery timing).
after_discharge.6_weeks: Fistulotomy wounds should be nearly healed. Plan stage 2 surgery (LIFT, advancement flap) if seton present. MRI to reassess if complex.
after_discharge.3_months: Confirm complete healing. Assess for incontinence with validated questionnaire (Wexner score). Discharge if fully healed and asymptomatic.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: severe pain, fever, swelling, heavy bleeding, new discharge, inability to pass urine, or if seton falls out or causes severe discomfort.
